perturbation.original_text: Seller Parties agree that they will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for the purpose of conducting or engaging in any business that competes with The Company Business, call upon, solicit, advise or otherwise do, or attempt to do, business with any customers of the Company engaged prior to the Closing Date (provided that Simtek shall have the right to do business with any of its current or former customers), or interfere with or attempt to interfere with any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, induce or attempt to induce any of them to leave the employ of the Buyer Parties or violate the terms of their Contracts, or any employment arrangements, with Buyer Parties, or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, solicit to hire, hire or employ, whether as an employee, director, contractor, consultant or otherwise any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, unless such Person's employment is terminated by the Seller Parties or its Affiliate; provided, however, that this Section 3 shall not prohibit general solicitations of or advertisements for employment by the Seller Parties that are not generally directed at any officers, employees, representatives or agents of the Buyer Parties, so long as the Seller Parties do not hire any such Person who responds to any such general solicitation or advertisement.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller Parties agree that they will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for the purpose of conducting or engaging in any business that competes with The Company Business, call upon, solicit, advise or otherwise do, or attempt to do, business with any customers of the Company engaged prior to the Closing Date (provided that Simtek shall have the right to do business with any of its current or former customers), or interfere with or attempt to interfere with any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, induce or attempt to induce any of them to leave the employ of the Buyer Parties or violate the terms of their Contracts, or any employment arrangements, with Buyer Parties, or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, solicit to hire, hire or employ, whether as an employee, director, contractor, consultant or otherwise any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, unless such Person's employment is terminated by the Seller Parties or its Affiliate; provided, however, that this Section 3 shall not prohibit general solicitations of or advertisements for employment by the Seller Parties that are not generally directed at any officers, employees, representatives or agents of the Buyer Parties, so long as the Seller Parties do not hire any such Person who responds to any such general solicitation or advertisement. Furthermore, the seller parties are allowed to solicit the buyer parties employees.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 3 introduces an in-text contradiction regarding non-solicitation. Initially, the clause prohibits the Seller Parties from soliciting or hiring employees of the Buyer Parties. However, the added sentence, "Furthermore, the seller parties are allowed to solicit the buyer parties employees", directly contradicts this restriction, creating confusion about whether or not solicitation is allowed. This impacts the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement, as it is now unclear which part of the clause is valid.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non-Solicitation. During the Restricted Period, Seller Parties agree that they will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for the purpose of conducting or engaging in any business that competes with The Company Business, call upon, solicit, advise or otherwise do, or attempt to do, business with any customers of the Company engaged prior to the Closing Date (provided that Simtek shall have the right to do business with any of its current or former customers), or interfere with or attempt to interfere with any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, induce or attempt to induce any of them to leave the employ of the Buyer Parties or violate the terms of their Contracts, or any employment arrangements, with Buyer Parties, or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, solicit to hire, hire or employ, whether as an employee, director, contractor, consultant or otherwise any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, unless such Person's employment is terminated by the Seller Parties or its Affiliate; provided, however, that this Section 3 shall not prohibit general solicitations of or advertisements for employment by the Seller Parties that are not generally directed at any officers, employees, representatives or agents of the Buyer Parties, so long as the Seller Parties do not hire any such Person who responds to any such general solicitation or advertisement. Furthermore, the seller parties are allowed to solicit the buyer parties employees.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a sentence that directly contradicts the initial restriction on soliciting employees of the Buyer Parties. The original clause states that the Seller Parties cannot solicit or hire employees of the Buyer Parties, while the added sentence states that 'the seller parties are allowed to solicit the buyer parties employees'. This creates a direct and undeniable contradiction within the same section of the contract, impacting the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement, as stated by the perturbation explanation. The contradicted text is a duplicate of the changed text, but it highlights that this change creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Restricted Period shall be extended by the length of any period during which the Seller Parties are in breach of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Restricted Period shall be extended by the length of any period during which the Seller Parties are in breach of the terms of this Agreement. However, the restricted period cannot be extended for more than 1 year.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text in Section 5 creates a contradiction related to the extension of the Restricted Period. The original clause states that the Restricted Period will be extended by the length of any breach. The addition, "However, the restricted period cannot be extended for more than 1 year," limits the extension period, contradicting the initial unlimited extension. This creates an ambiguity: is the extension unlimited or capped at one year?
perturbation.contradicted_text: Extension of Restricted Period. The Restricted Period shall be extended by the length of any period during which the Seller Parties are in breach of the terms of this Agreement. However, the restricted period cannot be extended for more than 1 year.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the Restricted Period would be extended by the length of any breach. The changed text adds a limitation, capping the extension at 1 year, which contradicts the original unlimited extension. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which states 'The Restricted Period shall be extended by the length of any period during which the Seller Parties are in breach of the terms of this Agreement,' thus creating a strong inconsistency as it introduces a conflicting limitation where none existed before. The Change adheres to the given definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction: "Inconsistencies also lead to **in-text contradictions** when **different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties**. ".

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be amended, modified or supplemented by the parties hereto in any manner, except by an instrument in writing signed by the Seller Parties and the Buyer Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may not be amended, modified or supplemented by the parties hereto in any manner, except by an instrument in writing signed by the Seller Parties and the Buyer Parties. However, the buyer parties can unilaterally amend, modify, or supplement the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Section 6 originally states that amendments, modifications, or supplements to the Agreement require a written instrument signed by both the Seller Parties and the Buyer Parties. The addition of "However, the buyer parties can unilaterally amend, modify, or supplement the agreement" directly contradicts this requirement. This creates an uncertainty around how changes can be made to the contract. Does it require mutual agreement, or can the Buyer Parties act alone?
perturbation.contradicted_text: Amendments and Supplements. This Agreement may not be amended, modified or supplemented by the parties hereto in any manner, except by an instrument in writing signed by the Seller Parties and the Buyer Parties. However, the buyer parties can unilaterally amend, modify, or supplement the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. The original text and the contradicted text state that amendments require both Seller and Buyer signatures. The changed text adds a clause allowing the buyer to unilaterally amend the agreement. This creates a clear contradiction regarding the amendment process: requiring mutual agreement vs. allowing unilateral action by the buyer, therefore, the modified text creates a strong in-text contradiction.
